Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, dedicating about four to five hours a day spread across the week. I'd usually block out mornings for reading and note-taking since that's when I focus best, then use evenings for reviewing what I'd written. Having 15 online courses meant I needed to be organized, so I kept a planner to track what needed attention each week. It wasn't perfect, but I made it work most of the time.
Q2: Honestly, my pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due. I'd aim for regular study times, but when deadlines piled up, everything went out the window and I'd just focus on whatever was most urgent. Some weeks I was super disciplined, others I was scrambling. The inconsistency stressed me out, but with so many courses running simultaneously, it was hard to maintain perfect regularity.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check for new announcements or see if any deadlines were coming up. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post or new material being uploaded, and that would prompt me to jump on. I also logged in pretty regularly just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important across all my courses. It became almost like a habit to check in even when I didn't have a specific task.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became pretty automatic for me. I'd wake up, make coffee, and check the platform before doing anything else. It happened naturally because I was juggling so many online courses and couldn't afford to miss updates. The discussion posts also kept me coming back since I wanted to see what others were saying and contribute when I could.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and take detailed notes, then rewrite key concepts in my own words. Discussion forums were also helpful because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. The structured written content just clicked better with my learning style than videos did.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't watch videos as much as I probably should have. I'd skim through them or watch at 2x speed, but they just didn't stick for me the way reading did. I also tended to ignore some of the optional supplementary materials when I was overwhelmed with required work. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, I just had to prioritize and my energy was limited.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I'd be assessed on or when it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. If I finished something and felt like I'd actually learned or could apply it, that was worthwhile. Activities that felt like busywork or repetition of things I already knew got pushed aside. I needed to see the payoff, especially with my workload.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. With required work, there was this pressure that kept me going even when motivation was low. Optional stuff felt more like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When I did engage with optional content, it was because I was genuinely curious, which made it more enjoyable but also easier to skip when things got hectic. I wish I'd done more of them honestly.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded pretty much everything I could and studied from those files offline constantly. I'd print out readings, annotate them by hand, and create written summaries in notebooks. A lot of my actual learning happened with a pen and paper, away from the screen. I also used external resources like academic articles and textbooks that weren't part of the official course materials.
Q10: There were many times I spent hours working through downloaded materials or rewriting notes without touching the LMS at all. I'd have the PDFs saved on my laptop and study from those for days between logins. My best study sessions were often completely offline, just me and my notes at the library. The LMS would show nothing, but I was definitely putting in serious work.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a reading once, download it immediately, and then never open it on the platform again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet or getting distracted by notifications. Some of those materials I revisited five or six times offline, but the system would only show that single initial access.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I had multiple courses with overlapping deadlines, and even though I was putting in over 30 hours a week, it never felt like enough. My motivation dipped pretty hard during that period. The CBT sessions I had helped me manage the stress, but there were definitely weeks where I was just going through the motions rather than truly engaging with the material.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log in but not really absorb anything. I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but my brain wasn't processing it. Competing responsibilities from extracurriculars and life stuff meant some weeks the LMS got minimal attention even though I was technically keeping up. Confusion with some course content also made me avoid certain modules temporarily.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have readings open on my screen but I was just staring at the words without taking anything in. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I couldn't remember a single point. Especially when stress was high, I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. Those sessions felt wasted, but I didn't always have the energy to do better.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Sometimes I'd avoid a task because I was anxious about not doing it well enough. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was harder than understanding it. My assignment completion rate definitely suffered because of this.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd ramp up activity significantly in the days before something was due, then drop off once it was submitted. It wasn't ideal, but that urgency was often what pushed me to finally engage with materials I'd been putting off. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out more evenly, but they also gave me necessary structure.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going and engage more with similar content. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to improve, but other times it just added to my stress and made me avoid that area for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback the most because it gave me something concrete to work with rather than just a grade.
Q18: The course structure helped me stay on track even when motivation was low. Having clear modules and deadlines meant I always knew what needed to be done next. The discussion requirements pushed me to engage with peers, which I might not have done otherwise. But sometimes the structure felt rigid, and I wished I could move at my own pace through certain sections.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for me since it fit my study style. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged real interaction. What made things harder was when too many things were due at once across different courses, or when instructions weren't clear. Also, some video-heavy sections didn't work well for how I learn.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. They might be reading, watching, and engaging with ideas but hitting a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. It could also be perfectionism or anxiety making it hard to submit work even after putting in lots of effort. High activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. I spent tons of time studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. My LMS activity probably looked moderate, but my actual engagement with the content was much higher. Someone could learn deeply from materials accessed once if they're studying them thoroughly offline. The platform can't capture everything that counts as real learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, what resources were accessed, and participation in discussions. They're good at tracking deadlines and completion rates. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and how much someone actually understood versus just clicked through. They can't measure whether someone was truly focused or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm asking questions, making connections to other things I know, and thinking about the content even when I'm not studying. Real engagement is when learning feels active rather than passive. It's not about time spent but about mental investment and genuine curiosity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed materials and handwritten notes, which was most of my actual learning. It wouldn't capture the mental effort I put into understanding concepts or the anxiety I pushed through to keep going. My participation in study groups with classmates outside the platform, the CBT sessions that helped me cope, and the genuine interest I developed in certain topics—none of that shows up in the data.
